html:
OMCC: a going-dark rural fiber asset priced like a failed finance shell

Old Market Capital is still screened as a tiny credit-services company, but the old Nicholas Financial business is gone. OMCC sold the auto-loan receivables to Westlake, stopped originating loans, and is now a holding company built around Amplex, a Northwest and North Central Ohio broadband operator. The latest 10-K says OMCC owned 56.5% of Amplex Holdings at March 31, 2025, with Amplex serving roughly 13,000 broadband customers, including 4,400 fiber subscribers and more than 12,000 fiber passings. By the September 2025 10-Q, that had moved to about 13,400 broadband customers, more than 5,000 fiber subscribers, and roughly 15,000 fiber passings.

The market is focused on the wrong facts. It sees an OTCID microcap that voluntarily delisted from Nasdaq, filed Form 15, stopped SEC reporting, has negative GAAP earnings, and trades maybe $15,000 to $20,000 a day. That is why the stock is at $4.10, down 22% over the last year, with a $28 million market cap. The variant view is that the delisting is not just a governance discount. It is also an economic catalyst. OMCC’s corporate costs were absurdly large relative to its size, and the board authorized a $7 million repurchase program in December 2025, equal to roughly 25% of the current market cap. Buying stock at roughly half of book value is far more accretive than most small-cap “growth” plans.

The operating signal is better than the GAAP screen. In the September quarter, revenue was $3.16 million, up 7% year over year, with fiber revenue up 31% while wireless revenue declined 6%. That is exactly the transition an investor should want: lower-value wireless customers being supplemented or replaced by higher-quality fiber relationships. The telecom segment was only slightly profitable on an operating-income basis because depreciation is ramping as plant goes into service, but segment EBITDA was roughly $0.9 million in the quarter before corporate overhead. Annualized, that is a $3 million to $3.5 million EBITDA rural broadband business still early in fiber penetration.

The balance sheet is the margin of safety. At September 30, 2025, OMCC had $18.9 million of cash, $39.7 million of net PP&E, $53.8 million of shareholder equity, and only $3.5 million of debt, most of it low-cost RUS financing. The company later invested another $4 million into Amplex, so corporate cash is lower, but that cash was moved into the controlled broadband asset rather than burned. At the current market cap, investors are paying roughly 0.5x book and about 0.7x tangible book for a company whose assets are mostly cash and fiber plant, not receivables from the old subprime auto lender.

The RUS loan is the hidden forward lever. Amplex has access to up to $21.3 million of secured RUS ReConnect financing at 2%, with principal and interest payments deferrable until October 2027 and maturity in 2046. That is not normal market-rate debt. It lets a tiny operator build rural fiber with long-duration, subsidized capital in markets where overbuild economics are unattractive for larger cable and fiber players. If Amplex can convert new passings into subscribers, the incremental economics should look much better than the consolidated historical financials.

The retail context is almost nonexistent, which is part of the opportunity. There is no meme narrative, no meaningful stock-promotion ecosystem, and no liquid shareholder base. The public conversation is mostly automated EDGAR and insider-filing posts. That makes OMCC nearly unownable for institutions, but it also means the January 2026 OTC transition likely created forced or indifferent selling rather than a considered reassessment of intrinsic value.

The bear case is real. Going dark reduces transparency. Management still says it may pursue additional controlling investments in other sectors, which creates blank-check capital-allocation risk. Amplex is small, rural, and capital-intensive. If fiber passings do not convert, if BEAD-funded competitors overbuild the same pockets, or if the company stops communicating with minority shareholders, the discount can persist indefinitely.

But the setup is asymmetric because the current quote already prices in a lot of that. The stock does not need a heroic takeout to work. It needs some combination of basic voluntary disclosure, visible buyback execution, steady fiber subscriber growth, and evidence that growth capex is creating cash flow rather than just plant on the balance sheet. A $7 million buyback around current prices could retire roughly one quarter of the company and mechanically lift book value per share. That is the cleanest near-term source of torque.

At the May 15, 2026 close of $4.10, my targets are:

Bear: $2.75, down 33%. No useful disclosure, poor buyback execution, Amplex consumes cash, and the stock trades like a dark, illiquid holding company.

Base: $8.25, up 101%. The buyback is at least partly executed, fiber growth continues, corporate costs fall after deregistration, and the stock re-rates toward 0.8x to 0.9x post-buyback book value.

Bull: $12.50, up 205%. OMCC aggressively repurchases stock, Amplex proves that RUS-funded fiber builds earn attractive returns, fiber EBITDA scales, and the market or a strategic buyer values the asset as a scarce rural broadband platform rather than a failed auto lender.
